dialogue.utterance: Dispatch unit 3 to the residence for reports of abdominal pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What made you call 911 today, ma'am?
I have stomach pain all over. It's sharp and crampy, like a 10 out of 10.
Can you tell me your name and where you are now?
My name is Lena, and I'm in [CITY].
Do you know what day it is and why you called 911?
Wednesday. I called because of all-over stomach pain. It started yesterday afternoon at work.
I'm going to take your blood pressure now, please sit still.
Your blood pressure is 108 over 69. Now I'll check your glucose.
Your glucose is 189. Are you comfortable lying back so I can check your oxygen saturation?
Your oxygen saturation is 97-this is good. Are you having any trouble with your breathing?
Your chest movements look normal and regular. Any dizziness or shortness of breath?
Breathing is okay. I did throw up once and had some nausea and diarrhea.
My radial pulse is 104, strong and regular. Any chest pain or lightheadedness?
No chest pain or dizziness. Just some chills.

Can you tell me a bit more about the nature of your pain today? 17. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Any other symptoms like fever, blood in vomit, or blood in your stool or urine? 18. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: No blood, no fever. Just chills off and on most of the day.
Do you have any long-term medical conditions we should know about?
I have GERD-nothing else medically.
Can I have your age and gender for our records?

Thirty-five and female. That's in my emergency file too. 23. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: onset; EMT: When did your pain first start? 24. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: onset; Patient: Around 3:00 PM yesterday, while I was at work.
How would you describe your pain?
Sharp and crampy for sure. It's all over my belly.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how severe is the pain right now? 28. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: severity; Patient: It's a solid 10. It hasn't gone down anywhere on my stomach.
Is the pain moving anywhere else, like to your back or sides? 30. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: radiation; Patient: No, it's centered right here, no movement.
Your skin looks normal and feels fine-warm and dry.
Your airway is patent. You're speaking clearly and staying alert.
You're oriented to time, place, person, and event-well done.
Any issues with your urine, such as pain or frequency?
Nothing there. Same with stool, nothing bloody. Just threw up once with green.
Your abdomen is soft and not tender when I gently press it.
You're fully alert. Your GCS score is 15.
The EKG shows a normal rhythm. Let me check your O2 and pulse again.
Your breathing rate is 14, and depth looks fine.
Your heart rate remains at 104. Strong and regular.
We are going to attach a 4-lead EKG to monitor your heartbeat.
We placed the 4-lead EKG. You might feel some pressure but shouldn't be uncomfortable.
IV access has been established in your left arm with a 20g IV and flushed with saline.
We have given you 4mg of IV Zofran to help with the nausea.
Your IV included a 10 mL saline flush before and after the Zofran to protect the line.
You've received 100 mL of normal saline through your IV to help keep you hydrated.
We are following the protocol for nausea and vomiting. Treatments are in effect.
Your heart rate has gone down to 78. Feels more comfortable now?
A bit better. Still feel shaky, but pain is a little less.
Your oxygen is up to 98. Vitals are definitely trending better.
Blood pressure now is 112 over 63.
Your breathing rate has increased to 16, with normal depth and rhythm.
Let me do another quick EKG check.
EKG remains stable with a normal sinus rhythm.
Would you be comfortable being taken to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine. I'm okay with that.
We'll transport you non-emergent using the left AC IV line. You'll be safe and well-secured.
Patient is secured using a 5-point harness. We're ready.
We're on the way to the emergency department. It'll be about 15 minutes.
Patient is taken to bed 22 and our full report has been shared with hospital staff.
TO callsign [COMPANY_NAME] receiving patient for follow-up abdominal pain protocoled for nausea and vomiting.
I am your transport crew. Everything is secure and stable.
Final check of vitals-BP 112/63, 16 breaths, and a strong 78.
IV line is patent, 20g in left AC with 100 mL of normal saline given so far.
Remember 4mg Zofran administered via IV with 10 mL saline flush both before and after.
Patient is GCS 15 and has been alert the whole time during transport.
Patient is sitting with 5-point harness, currently very stable.
Patient's oxygen is 98%. Everything stays well within normal and safe ranges.
Road conditions look good, and the ride should be smooth for our patient.
Noticing patient's signs and symptoms are unchanged since the last assessment.
EKG throughout transport remained in normal sinus rhythm with no changes.
Glucose is normal, no signs of instability or deterioration.
The left arm remains well-secured with no signs of IV infiltrations or discomfort.
Our patient has a confirmed medical history only of GERD nothing more complex, and no allergies.
Patient is 35, female, with full autonomy and cooperation on arrival and throughout EMS package.
We had no incidents during transport. All rounds of reassessment are within tolerances.
Historically, patient reported no prior medications in use and no emergency issues aside from GERD.
TOD in protocol 3-9, nausea-and vomiting car for [COMPANY_NAME] delivery.
All entries here from EPCR are fully accounted and transcribed from initial arrival to final handoff.
Transport mission non-emergent, no significant interventions beyond those already done.
All blood products, medications, lables, and delivery dimensions accurate up to point of transfer.
No residual risks from VPRO-IV fluid feeding clear, flow unobstructed during entire travel session.
Complete care bundle followed at on-site and en rout with complete PPE worn by crew.
Final vital signs recorded and verified with last electronic record at destination.
We are now presenting patient for intake in good alignment and condition.
Patient is conscious, oriented 4/4 per GCS, and currently demonstrating no distress as expected.
Movement well-controlled, and IV not compromised during curvy trip of transport ambu to hospital.
EKG and all baseline checks stable during initial, regular, and final assessment times.
Patient loadout complete and intact, including all personal belongings and hospital note.
Bring our phone and water bottle into the ED while the rest should be stocked in our system.
We have no interruptions in care and responsibility formally transferred to [COMPANY_NAME].
Assignment finalized by both [COMPANY_NAME] and capitated by our final paper copy completion today.
Final checks completed at 11:27 as per documentation with only no further actions required.
We know GPS shows arrival before 11:27 as scheduled with full transparency now official.
All timestamps, product names, and total liquid quantity correctly labeled per protocol.
Thank you for receiving Lena on our behalf. All details ready for [COMPANY_NAME] admission team.
